strengths: **Healthy real-world volume** — This showcase is derived from a real multi-agent telemetry snapshot, not fabricated toy numbers.
**Low tool failure rate** (691 failures out of 16007 tool calls = 4.3%) — Indicates well-formed requests with clear context.
**Effective subagent delegation** — Used 227 subagent launches across 8 agent type(s), keeping the main conversation focused and enabling parallel investigation.
**Highly productive editing sessions** — 0.9 file edits per prompt. Prompts translate directly into code changes, showing clear intent.
observations: Agent activity is spread across multiple workflows, which makes comparison and session-level diagnostics immediately valuable.
Context gathering looks controlled relative to prompt volume.
691 tool failures detected (4.3% of tool calls). Path and schema validation up front can reduce iteration cost.
Subagents are used for deeper workflows (227 launches). Provide explicit deliverable formats in subagent prompts for cleaner outputs.
59 session(s) look context-heavy (many prompts, many events, or large token volume). Long-lived sessions are where token accumulation quietly compounds.
recommendations: Open Compare first: choose one agent, then read token share, tool ratio, and failure rate against the rest of the cohort before changing prompts or models.
Use a fixed prompt contract for non-trivial requests: Goal, Context, Constraints, Output, Done-when.
Pin relevant files/folders in the first prompt to reduce exploratory tool churn.
For MCP and path-sensitive tasks, require schema/path check as step one before execution.
For medium/large tasks, request two phases explicitly: plan first, execute second.
practical_examples: Use comparison to spot budget drift
Which agent is quietly getting more expensive?
Filter to the last 7 days, compare one agent against the rest of the cohort, and inspect token share, tool ratio, and failure rate before changing prompts or models.
Turn failures into a guided debug loop
Open the failing session, inspect tool failures and shell activity, then compare it against a healthy cohort to see what changed in execution shape.
Use the session drawer as a handoff view
What happened in this implementation pass?
Review the session timeline, key tools, token shape, and completion signals so a teammate can pick up the thread without replaying the whole transcript.
